topics.label: Logs, roots, and exponents
topics.description: Natural logs, base-2 logs, square roots, exponentials, and mixed approximation drills.

topics.label: Normal tails
topics.description: Memorized tail anchors and reverse-quantile questions.

topics.label: Expected max
topics.description: Use the 1/n tail quantile as an anchor, then remember the mean sits a bit above it.

topics.label: Discrete extremes
topics.description: Geometric and Poisson max/min questions plus full-set occupancy approximations.

topics.label: Cards and sampling
topics.description: Deck, urn, and without-replacement questions built on indicators and symmetry.

topics.label: Conditional probability
topics.description: Posterior updates, Bayes questions, hidden-state inference, and conditional sample-space drills.

topics.label: Moments and mgf
topics.description: Second moments, variance, covariance, correlation, and mgf drills.

topics.label: Basic review
topics.description: Mean, variance, and selected higher moments for standard distribution families.

topics.label: Regression
topics.description: OLS, regression MLE, residual variance, prediction, and Bayesian coefficient updates.

topics.label: Order statistics
topics.description: Uniform, dice, and exponential top-k / bottom-k estimate drills.

topics.label: Estimation and MLE
topics.description: Likelihood-based parameter recovery, sample summaries, and fast inference rules.

topics.label: Fisher information
topics.description: Information, Cramer-Rao bounds, and large-sample MLE variance / standard-error drills.

topics.label: Options pricing
topics.description: Put-call parity, synthetic forwards, binomial pricing, and intrinsic-versus-time-value drills.

topics.label: Fixed income
topics.description: Present value, coupon bonds, annuities, perpetuities, and simple yield-based pricing drills.

topics.label: Greeks
topics.description: Delta, hedge sizing, vega intuition, and option-position sensitivity questions.

topics.label: Ito and GBM
topics.description: Apply Ito's lemma to simple transforms of geometric Brownian motion.

topics.label: Stopping and martingales
topics.description: Threshold rerolls, continuation values, and bounded optional-stopping classics.

topics.label: Vol and Sharpe scaling
topics.description: Sqrt-time scaling for volatility and Sharpe ratios.

topics.label: Sharpe ratio
topics.description: Estimate Sharpe from cumulative PnL charts, plus Sharpe-to-probability and Sharpe-bound drills.

topics.label: Expected value
topics.description: Quick payoff-weighting, conditioning, and indicator-count questions.

topics.label: Random walks on Z
topics.description: Gambler's ruin, exit time, hitting-time variance, and return-probability shortcuts.

topics.label: Symmetric polygons
topics.description: Cycle walks and regular-polygon geometry with closed-form anchors.

topics.label: Markov chain spectra
topics.description: Transition-matrix eigenvalues and spectral gaps for simple random walks on rings.

topics.label: Classic puzzles
topics.description: Noodles, runs, streaks, permutations, derangements, and coupon-collector staples.

topics.label: Miscellaneous custom cards
topics.description: A catch-all topic for saved custom cards that do not fit the built-in categories.

normal.prompt: For Z ~ N(0, 1), estimate P(Z > 0.84).
normal.hint: Use the memorized upper-tail anchors from the normal table.
normal.explanation: The exact upper tail at z = 0.84 is about 20%, while the mental-table anchor is 20%.

normal.prompt: For Z ~ N(0, 1), estimate P(Z > 1.28).
normal.hint: Use the memorized upper-tail anchors from the normal table.
normal.explanation: The exact upper tail at z = 1.28 is about 10%, while the mental-table anchor is 10%.

normal.prompt: For Z ~ N(0, 1), estimate P(Z > 1.64).
normal.hint: Use the memorized upper-tail anchors from the normal table.
normal.explanation: The exact upper tail at z = 1.64 is about 5.1%, while the mental-table anchor is 5%.

normal.prompt: For Z ~ N(0, 1), estimate P(Z > 1.96).
normal.hint: Use the memorized upper-tail anchors from the normal table.
normal.explanation: The exact upper tail at z = 1.96 is about 2.5%, while the mental-table anchor is 2.5%.

normal.prompt: For Z ~ N(0, 1), estimate P(Z > 2.33).
normal.hint: Use the memorized upper-tail anchors from the normal table.
normal.explanation: The exact upper tail at z = 2.33 is about 0.99%, while the mental-table anchor is 1%.

normal.prompt: For Z ~ N(0, 1), estimate P(Z > 2.58).
normal.hint: Use the memorized upper-tail anchors from the normal table.
normal.explanation: The exact upper tail at z = 2.58 is about 0.49%, while the mental-table anchor is 0.5%.

normal.prompt: For Z ~ N(0, 1), estimate P(Z > 3.09).
normal.hint: Use the memorized upper-tail anchors from the normal table.
normal.explanation: The exact upper tail at z = 3.09 is about 0.1%, while the mental-table anchor is 0.1%.

normal.prompt: Find z so that P(Z > z) ~= 20% for Z ~ N(0, 1).
normal.hint: Reverse the same standard tail table.
normal.explanation: The exact quantile for tail 20% is about 0.8416, while the common mental anchor is 0.84.

normal.prompt: Find z so that P(Z > z) ~= 10% for Z ~ N(0, 1).
normal.hint: Reverse the same standard tail table.
normal.explanation: The exact quantile for tail 10% is about 1.2816, while the common mental anchor is 1.28.

normal.prompt: Find z so that P(Z > z) ~= 5% for Z ~ N(0, 1).
normal.hint: Reverse the same standard tail table.
normal.explanation: The exact quantile for tail 5% is about 1.6449, while the common mental anchor is 1.64.

normal.prompt: Find z so that P(Z > z) ~= 2.5% for Z ~ N(0, 1).
normal.hint: Reverse the same standard tail table.
normal.explanation: The exact quantile for tail 2.5% is about 1.96, while the common mental anchor is 1.96.

normal.prompt: Find z so that P(Z > z) ~= 1% for Z ~ N(0, 1).
normal.hint: Reverse the same standard tail table.
normal.explanation: The exact quantile for tail 1% is about 2.3263, while the common mental anchor is 2.33.

normal.prompt: Find z so that P(Z > z) ~= 0.5% for Z ~ N(0, 1).
normal.hint: Reverse the same standard tail table.
normal.explanation: The exact quantile for tail 0.5% is about 2.5758, while the common mental anchor is 2.58.

normal.prompt: Find z so that P(Z > z) ~= 0.1% for Z ~ N(0, 1).
normal.hint: Reverse the same standard tail table.
normal.explanation: The exact quantile for tail 0.1% is about 3.0902, while the common mental anchor is 3.09.

normal.prompt: X, Y, and Z are jointly standard normal with pairwise correlations all equal to 0.5. Estimate P(X + Y > Z + 2).
normal.hint: A linear combination of jointly normal variables is normal. First compute the variance of X + Y - Z.
normal.explanation: Let W = X + Y - Z. Then Var(W) = 1 + 1 + 1 + 2(0.5) - 2(0.5) - 2(0.5) = 2, so W ~ N(0, 2). The target probability is P(W > 2) = P(N(0,1) > sqrt(2)), about 7.9%.

normal.prompt: X, Y, and Z are jointly standard normal with every pairwise correlation equal to 0.25. Estimate P(X + Y > Z + 1.5).
normal.hint: Compute the variance of X + Y - Z, then convert the event to a 1-dimensional normal tail.
normal.explanation: Let W = X + Y - Z. Then Var(W) = 3 - 2ρ = 2.5, so the event becomes P(N(0,1) > 0.9487).

normal.prompt: X, Y, and Z are jointly standard normal with every pairwise correlation equal to 0.4. Estimate P(X + Y > Z + 2).
normal.hint: Compute the variance of X + Y - Z, then convert the event to a 1-dimensional normal tail.
normal.explanation: Let W = X + Y - Z. Then Var(W) = 3 - 2ρ = 2.2, so the event becomes P(N(0,1) > 1.3484).

normal.prompt: X, Y, and Z are jointly standard normal with every pairwise correlation equal to 0.5. Estimate P(X + Y > Z + 2.5).
normal.hint: Compute the variance of X + Y - Z, then convert the event to a 1-dimensional normal tail.
normal.explanation: Let W = X + Y - Z. Then Var(W) = 3 - 2ρ = 2, so the event becomes P(N(0,1) > 1.7678).

normal.prompt: X, Y, and Z are jointly standard normal with every pairwise correlation equal to 0.6. Estimate P(X + Y > Z + 1.8).
normal.hint: Compute the variance of X + Y - Z, then convert the event to a 1-dimensional normal tail.
normal.explanation: Let W = X + Y - Z. Then Var(W) = 3 - 2ρ = 1.8, so the event becomes P(N(0,1) > 1.3416).

normal.prompt: X, Y, and Z are jointly standard normal with every pairwise correlation equal to 0.75. Estimate P(X + Y > Z + 1).
normal.hint: Compute the variance of X + Y - Z, then convert the event to a 1-dimensional normal tail.
normal.explanation: Let W = X + Y - Z. Then Var(W) = 3 - 2ρ = 1.5, so the event becomes P(N(0,1) > 0.8165).

normal.explanation: By symmetry, P(Z < -0.84) = P(Z > 0.84).

normal.hint: Use symmetry: the left tail below -z equals the upper tail above z.
normal.explanation: Use symmetry between the lower left tail and the upper right tail.

normal.prompt: For Z ~ N(0, 1), estimate P(Z < 0.52).
normal.hint: A lower-tail probability is 1 minus the upper tail above the same z-score.

normal.hint: Take 1 minus the two-sided tail.
normal.explanation: The centered mass is 1 minus the two-sided tail.

normal.prompt: Find z so that P(Z > z) ~= 30% for Z ~ N(0, 1).

normal.prompt: Estimate P(|Z| < 3.09) for Z standard normal.
normal.hint: Use the two equal tails outside +/-z.
normal.explanation: Subtract both tails outside +/-z from 1.

normal.prompt: Estimate P(|Z| > 3.09) for Z standard normal.
normal.hint: Double the one-sided upper tail.
normal.explanation: Use symmetry: one tail on each side.

normal.prompt: Estimate z such that P(Z > z) is about 0.1%.
normal.hint: This is one of the standard upper-tail anchors.
normal.explanation: Map the tail probability back to its memorized z-score anchor.

normal.prompt: Estimate z such that P(|Z| < z) is about 99.8%.
normal.hint: Convert the central probability into one tail on each side.
normal.explanation: A central area of c leaves tail area (1-c)/2 on each side.

normal.hint: Subtract two upper-tail anchors or two CDF anchors.
normal.explanation: The mass between the two z-levels is the difference between their CDF values, or equivalently the difference between their upper tails.

normal.hint: Double the one-sided band between the two positive z-values.

maxNormals.prompt: Estimate E[max of 5 iid N(0, 1) draws].
maxNormals.hint: Use P(M <= x) = Phi(x)^n and the small-tail approximation (1 - p)^n ~= e^(-np); the 1-exceedance rule gives a fast anchor, not the exact mean.
maxNormals.explanation: Let M be the max. Then P(M <= x) = Phi(x)^5. If p = P(Z > x) is small, this is about (1 - p)^5 ~= e^(-5p). Setting 5p ~= 1 gives p ~= 20%, so x is the 20% upper-tail normal anchor, about 0.84. That point is where the max is already above x with probability about 1 - e^(-1) ~= 63%, so it is a central location estimate rather than the exact mean. The true E[max] is about 1.163, a bit above the shortcut anchor.

maxNormals.prompt: Estimate E[max of 2 iid N(0, 1) draws].
maxNormals.hint: Use max(X,Y) = (X + Y + |X - Y|)/2 and X - Y ~ N(0,2).
maxNormals.explanation: Write max(X,Y) = (X + Y + |X - Y|)/2. Since X and Y are centered normals, E[X + Y] = 0. Also X - Y ~ N(0,2), so E|X - Y| = sqrt(2) × sqrt(2/pi) = 2/sqrt(pi). Divide by 2 to get E[max(X,Y)] = 1/sqrt(pi) ≈ 0.5642.

maxNormals.prompt: Estimate E[min of 2 iid N(0, 1) draws].
maxNormals.hint: Use min(X,Y) = (X + Y - |X - Y|)/2 or symmetry with the max.
maxNormals.explanation: Either use min(X,Y) = (X + Y - |X - Y|)/2 or symmetry. The max has mean 1/sqrt(pi), so the min has mean -1/sqrt(pi) ≈ -0.5642.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Let G1, ..., G8 be iid geometric(1/2), counting trials until first success. Estimate E[max G_i].
discreteExtremes.hint: Use P(G >= k) = 2^(-(k - 1)) and solve nP(G >= k) ~= 1; the exact mean sits a bit above that cutoff.
discreteExtremes.explanation: For geometric(1/2), P(G >= k) = 2^(-(k - 1)). Setting 8 * 2^(-(k - 1)) ~= 1 gives k ~= 4, which is the central location of the max. The exact E[max] is about 4.4211, a bit above that anchor.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Let G1, ..., G16 be iid geometric(1/2), counting trials until first success. Estimate E[max G_i].
discreteExtremes.hint: Use P(G >= k) = 2^(-(k - 1)) and solve nP(G >= k) ~= 1; the exact mean sits a bit above that cutoff.
discreteExtremes.explanation: Here P(G >= k) = 2^(-(k - 1)), so 16 * 2^(-(k - 1)) ~= 1 gives k ~= 5. That is the one-exceedance anchor; the exact E[max] is about 5.3774.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Let G1, ..., G5 be iid geometric(10%), counting trials until first success. Estimate E[min G_i].
discreteExtremes.hint: The minimum of iid geometrics is geometric with success chance 1 - (1 - p)^n. For small p, a quick shortcut is to replace that by np.
discreteExtremes.explanation: The exact minimum is geometric with success chance 1 - 0.9^5 = 0.4095, so its mean is about 2.4419. A fast mental shortcut uses np = 0.5, giving a rough mean near 2.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Estimate E[max of 10 iid Poisson(16) variables].
discreteExtremes.hint: Approximate Poisson(16) by N(16, 16), then use a 10% upper-tail anchor because 10 * tail ~= 1.
discreteExtremes.explanation: Approximate each variable by N(16, 16), so the standard deviation is 4. The one-exceedance anchor uses a 10% upper tail, giving 16 + 1.28 * 4 = 21.12. As with other maxima, the exact mean sits a bit above that location, at about 22.4137.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Estimate E[min of 10 iid Poisson(16) variables].
discreteExtremes.hint: Approximate Poisson(16) by N(16, 16), then use a 10% lower-tail anchor because 10 * tail ~= 1.
discreteExtremes.explanation: With the normal approximation N(16, 16), the standard deviation is 4. A 10% lower-tail anchor gives 16 - 1.28 * 4 = 10.88. The exact E[min] is about 11.1629.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Estimate E[max of 20 iid Poisson(9) variables].
discreteExtremes.hint: Approximate Poisson(9) by N(9, 9), then use a 5% upper-tail anchor because 20 * tail ~= 1.
discreteExtremes.explanation: Here N(9, 9) gives mean 9 and standard deviation 3. The one-exceedance location uses a 5% upper tail, so the shortcut is 9 + 1.64 * 3 = 13.92. The true E[max] is higher, about 15.0155.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Estimate E[min of 20 iid Poisson(9) variables].
discreteExtremes.hint: Approximate Poisson(9) by N(9, 9), then use a 5% lower-tail anchor because 20 * tail ~= 1.
discreteExtremes.explanation: Using N(9, 9), the standard deviation is 3. A 5% lower-tail anchor gives 9 - 1.64 * 3 = 4.08. The exact E[min] is about 4.9252.

discreteExtremes.prompt: You throw 60 fair six-sided dice. Estimate the expected number of complete 1-through-6 sets you can remove.
discreteExtremes.hint: The number of removable full sets is the minimum face count. Each face count is roughly Bin(60, 1/6), with mean 10 and standard deviation about 2.89.
discreteExtremes.explanation: Write the answer as min(C1, ..., C6), where each face count has mean 10 and standard deviation about 2.8868. A quick min-of-normals shortcut uses a left-tail anchor around z ~= 0.97 because 6 * tail ~= 1, giving about 7.1999. The exact expected number of full sets is lower, about 6.185, because the discrete multinomial structure and dependence matter.

discreteExtremes.prompt: You throw 160 fair 20-sided dice. Estimate the expected number of complete 1-through-20 sets you can remove.
discreteExtremes.hint: Again the answer is the minimum face count. Each count is roughly Bin(160, 1/20), so center at 8 and use a 5% left-tail anchor because 20 * tail ~= 1.
discreteExtremes.explanation: Each face count has mean 8 and standard deviation about 2.7568. Since 20 * tail ~= 1 suggests a 5% normal anchor, the fast estimate is 8 - 1.64 * sd ~= 3.4788. The exact expected number of full 1-through-20 sets is about 3.2464.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Let G1, ..., G4 be iid geometric(1/2), counting trials until first success. Estimate E[max G_i].
discreteExtremes.hint: Use 4 times P(G >= k) ~= 1 with P(G >= k) = 2^(-(k - 1)).
discreteExtremes.explanation: Set 4 * 2^(-(k - 1)) ~= 1, which points to k near 3.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Let G1, ..., G32 be iid geometric(1/2), counting trials until first success. Estimate E[max G_i].
discreteExtremes.hint: Use 32 times P(G >= k) ~= 1 with P(G >= k) = 2^(-(k - 1)).
discreteExtremes.explanation: Set 32 * 2^(-(k - 1)) ~= 1, which points to k near 6.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Let G1, ..., G10 be iid geometric(1/3), counting trials until first success. Estimate E[max G_i].
discreteExtremes.hint: Use P(G >= k) = (2/3)^(k - 1) and solve 10 times that tail ~= 1.
discreteExtremes.explanation: The one-exceedance cutoff solves 10 * (2/3)^(k - 1) ~= 1, which lands near 7.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Let G1, ..., G8 be iid geometric(20%), counting trials until first success. Estimate E[min G_i].
discreteExtremes.hint: The minimum of iid geometrics is geometric with success chance 1 - (1 - p)^n.
discreteExtremes.explanation: The exact success chance is 1 - 0.8^8. Mentally, you can treat that as a high success chance a bit above 80%, giving a mean around 1.25.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Let G1, ..., G10 be iid geometric(5%), counting trials until first success. Estimate E[min G_i].
discreteExtremes.hint: Again the minimum is geometric with success chance 1 - (1 - p)^n, and np is a quick mental anchor.
discreteExtremes.explanation: The fast anchor uses np = 0.5, so the minimum should arrive in roughly 2 trials.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Estimate E[max of 5 iid Poisson(25) variables].
discreteExtremes.hint: Approximate Poisson(25) by N(25, 25), then use the 20% upper-tail anchor because 5 times tail ~= 1.
discreteExtremes.explanation: Use mean 25, standard deviation 5, and the 20% upper-tail anchor z ~= 0.84.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Estimate E[min of 5 iid Poisson(25) variables].
discreteExtremes.hint: Approximate Poisson(25) by N(25, 25), then use the 20% lower-tail anchor because 5 times tail ~= 1.
discreteExtremes.explanation: Use mean 25, standard deviation 5, and the 20% lower-tail anchor z ~= 0.84.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Estimate E[max of 40 iid Poisson(25) variables].
discreteExtremes.hint: Approximate Poisson(25) by N(25, 25), then use the 2.5% upper-tail anchor because 40 times tail ~= 1.
discreteExtremes.explanation: Use mean 25, standard deviation 5, and the 2.5% upper-tail anchor z ~= 1.96.

discreteExtremes.prompt: Estimate E[min of 40 iid Poisson(25) variables].
discreteExtremes.hint: Approximate Poisson(25) by N(25, 25), then use the 2.5% lower-tail anchor because 40 times tail ~= 1.
discreteExtremes.explanation: Use mean 25, standard deviation 5, and the 2.5% lower-tail anchor z ~= 1.96.

discreteExtremes.prompt: You throw 120 fair ten-sided dice. Estimate the expected number of complete 1-through-10 sets you can remove.
discreteExtremes.hint: The number of sets is the minimum face count. Each count is roughly Bin(120, 1/10).
discreteExtremes.explanation: Each face count has mean 12 and standard deviation about 3.29. A 10% left-tail anchor gives the rough minimum location.

discreteExtremes.prompt: You throw 50 fair five-sided dice. Estimate the expected number of complete 1-through-5 sets you can remove.
discreteExtremes.hint: Again the number of sets is the minimum face count, and each count is close to Bin(50, 1/5).
discreteExtremes.explanation: Each face count has mean 10 and standard deviation about 2.83. With 5 categories, a 20% left-tail anchor is a good shortcut.

discreteExtremes.explanation: Solve the one-exceedance rule for the geometric tail and use that level as the anchor.

cardsSampling.prompt: You shuffle a standard 52-card deck. Estimate the expected number of cards that end up in their original positions.
cardsSampling.hint: Use one indicator for each card being fixed.
cardsSampling.explanation: Each specific card lands back in its original position with probability 1/52. Add those 52 indicator expectations to get 1.

cardsSampling.prompt: A well-shuffled deck has 26 red and 26 black cards. Estimate the expected number of color changes as you move from card 1 to card 52.
cardsSampling.hint: Look at each adjacent pair and ask for the probability the colors differ.
cardsSampling.explanation: There are 51 adjacent pairs. For any given pair, the probability of a color change is 26/51, so the expected number of changes is 51 times 26/51 = 26.

cardsSampling.prompt: You draw 5 cards from a standard deck. Estimate the expected number of aces in your hand.
cardsSampling.hint: Use one indicator for each ace being present, or use sample size times population proportion.
cardsSampling.explanation: The expected ace count is 5 times the ace proportion 4/52, which is 5/13.

cardsSampling.prompt: You shuffle a standard deck. Estimate the expected position of the first ace.
cardsSampling.hint: For k special cards placed uniformly among n positions, the expected earliest position is (n + 1) / (k + 1).
cardsSampling.explanation: Think of the four ace positions as a random 4-subset of {1, ..., 52}. The expected minimum of those positions is (52 + 1) / (4 + 1) = 53/5 = 10.6.

cardsSampling.prompt: A bag starts with 10 red and 10 blue balls. After 10 draws without replacement, you know you have seen 2 more red balls than blue balls. Estimate the probability the next ball is blue.
cardsSampling.hint: Translate the information into the exact counts already seen, then look at what remains.
cardsSampling.explanation: If the first 10 draws had 2 more red than blue, then you must have seen 6 red and 4 blue. That leaves 4 red and 6 blue, so the next ball is blue with probability 6/10 = 0.6.

cardsSampling.prompt: You are dealt exactly 2 cards from a standard 52-card deck. Estimate the probability both cards are kings.
cardsSampling.hint: Use 4/52 for the first king and 3/51 for the second, or count two-card hands directly.

cardsSampling.prompt: A bag contains 2 red balls and 2 blue balls. Balls are drawn one at a time without replacement, and before each draw you guess the color. You earn 1 dollar for each correct guess. Estimate the optimal expected winnings.
cardsSampling.hint: At each stage, guess the color that is currently more common among the unseen balls and work backward.
cardsSampling.explanation: Dynamic programming gives the exact value 17/6 ≈ 2.833. The optimal rule is always to guess the more numerous remaining color; ties are neutral by symmetry.

cardsSampling.prompt: Four cards are shuffled face down: water, earth, wind, and fire. You turn them over one by one. You win if you see water and earth before ever seeing fire. Estimate the probability of winning.
cardsSampling.hint: Wind is irrelevant. Among water, earth, and fire, ask whether fire is last.
cardsSampling.explanation: Only the relative order of water, earth, and fire matters. You win exactly when fire is last among those three symbols, which happens with probability 1/3.

cardsSampling.prompt: You are dealt exactly 2 cards from a standard 52-card deck. Estimate the probability both cards are aces.
cardsSampling.hint: There are 4 cards of any fixed rank. Use 4/52 for the first and 3/51 for the second.
cardsSampling.explanation: For any fixed rank, the chance is (4/52)(3/51) = 1/221.

cardsSampling.explanation: Once you know how many of each color were removed, the next probability comes from the remaining composition.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen uniformly from an always-tail coin, a fair coin, and an always-heads coin. You then observe the toss sequence H. Estimate the probability the next toss is H.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. an always-tail coin: 1 × 0 = 0; a fair coin: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5; an always-heads coin: 1 × 1 = 1 After normalization, an always-tail coin has posterior 0; a fair coin has posterior 0.3333; an always-heads coin has posterior 0.6667 Then average the probability of future sequence H under that posterior. That gives 0.8333.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen uniformly from a coin with P(H) = 1/4, a fair coin, and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence HH. Estimate the probability the next toss is H.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 1 × 0.0625 = 0.0625; a fair coin: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 1 × 0.5625 = 0.5625 After normalization, a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.0714; a fair coin has posterior 0.2857; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.6429 Then average the probability of future sequence H under that posterior. That gives 0.6429.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen uniformly from a coin with P(H) = 1/4, a fair coin, and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence HT. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a fair coin.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 1 × 0.1875 = 0.1875; a fair coin: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 1 × 0.1875 = 0.1875 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.3; a fair coin has posterior 0.4; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.3 Therefore the posterior probability of a fair coin is 0.4.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 1:2:1 from a coin with P(H) = 1/4, a fair coin, and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence H. Estimate the probability the next 2 tosses are HH.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25; a fair coin: 2 × 0.5 = 1; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 1 × 0.75 = 0.75 After normalization, a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.125; a fair coin has posterior 0.5; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.375 Then average the probability of future sequence HH under that posterior. That gives 0.3438.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen uniformly from a coin with P(H) = 1/3 and a coin with P(H) = 2/3. You then observe the toss sequence HHT. Estimate the probability the next toss is H.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. a coin with P(H) = 1/3: 1 × 0.074074 = 0.074074; a coin with P(H) = 2/3: 1 × 0.148148 = 0.148148 After normalization, a coin with P(H) = 1/3 has posterior 0.3333; a coin with P(H) = 2/3 has posterior 0.6667 Then average the probability of future sequence H under that posterior. That gives 0.5556.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen uniformly from a coin with P(H) = 1/3 and a coin with P(H) = 2/3. You then observe the toss sequence TT. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a coin with P(H) = 1/3.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/3: 1 × 0.444444 = 0.444444; a coin with P(H) = 2/3: 1 × 0.111111 = 0.111111 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/3 has posterior 0.8; a coin with P(H) = 2/3 has posterior 0.2 Therefore the posterior probability of a coin with P(H) = 1/3 is 0.8.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 3:1 from a coin with P(H) = 1/4 and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence H. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a coin with P(H) = 3/4.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 3 × 0.25 = 0.75; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 1 × 0.75 = 0.75 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.5; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.5 Therefore the posterior probability of a coin with P(H) = 3/4 is 0.5.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 1:3 from a coin with P(H) = 1/4 and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence T. Estimate the probability the next toss is H.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 1 × 0.75 = 0.75; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 3 × 0.25 = 0.75 After normalization, a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.5; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.5 Then average the probability of future sequence H under that posterior. That gives 0.5.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen uniformly from an always-tail coin, a fair coin, and an always-heads coin. You then observe the toss sequence HT. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a fair coin.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. an always-tail coin: 1 × 0 = 0; a fair coin: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25; an always-heads coin: 1 × 0 = 0 Normalizing gives an always-tail coin has posterior 0; a fair coin has posterior 1; an always-heads coin has posterior 0 Therefore the posterior probability of a fair coin is 1.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 1:1:2 from a coin with P(H) = 1/4, a fair coin, and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence HTH. Estimate the probability the next toss is T.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 1 × 0.046875 = 0.046875; a fair coin: 1 × 0.125 = 0.125; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 2 × 0.140625 = 0.28125 After normalization, a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.1034; a fair coin has posterior 0.2759; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.6207 Then average the probability of future sequence T under that posterior. That gives 0.3707.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen uniformly from a fair die and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe a 6. Estimate the probability the next roll is a 6.
conditionalProb.hint: First form the posterior over the dice, then average the future 6-probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: Update the posterior over the candidate dice using prior × likelihood. a fair die: 1 × 0.166667 = 0.166667; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5 After normalization, a fair die has posterior 0.25; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.75 Averaging the probability of future sequence 6 under that posterior gives 0.4167.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen uniformly from a fair die and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe a 6 and a 6. Estimate the posterior probability the die was a die with P(6) = 1/2.
conditionalProb.hint: Treat “6” versus “not 6” exactly like heads versus tails: prior × likelihood, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The observation sequence only depends on each die through its probability of rolling a 6. So the posterior weights are prior × likelihood. a fair die: 1 × 0.027778 = 0.027778; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 Normalizing gives a fair die has posterior 0.1; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.9 Hence the posterior probability of a die with P(6) = 1/2 is 0.9.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen uniformly from a fair die, a die with P(6) = 1/3, and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe a 6 and not 6. Estimate the probability the next roll is a 6.
conditionalProb.hint: First form the posterior over the dice, then average the future 6-probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: Update the posterior over the candidate dice using prior × likelihood. a fair die: 1 × 0.138889 = 0.138889; a die with P(6) = 1/3: 1 × 0.222222 = 0.222222; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 After normalization, a fair die has posterior 0.2273; a die with P(6) = 1/3 has posterior 0.3636; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.4091 Averaging the probability of future sequence 6 under that posterior gives 0.3636.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen with prior weights 2:1 from a fair die and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe a 6. Estimate the posterior probability the die was a die with P(6) = 1/2.
conditionalProb.hint: Treat “6” versus “not 6” exactly like heads versus tails: prior × likelihood, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The observation sequence only depends on each die through its probability of rolling a 6. So the posterior weights are prior × likelihood. a fair die: 2 × 0.166667 = 0.333333; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5 Normalizing gives a fair die has posterior 0.4; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.6 Hence the posterior probability of a die with P(6) = 1/2 is 0.6.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen with prior weights 1:2:1 from a fair die, a die with P(6) = 1/3, and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe not 6 and not 6. Estimate the posterior probability the die was a fair die.
conditionalProb.hint: Treat “6” versus “not 6” exactly like heads versus tails: prior × likelihood, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The observation sequence only depends on each die through its probability of rolling a 6. So the posterior weights are prior × likelihood. a fair die: 1 × 0.694444 = 0.694444; a die with P(6) = 1/3: 2 × 0.444444 = 0.888889; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 Normalizing gives a fair die has posterior 0.3788; a die with P(6) = 1/3 has posterior 0.4848; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.1364 Hence the posterior probability of a fair die is 0.3788.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen uniformly from a fair die, a die with P(6) = 1/3, and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe a 6 and a 6. Estimate the probability the next 2 rolls are 6 and 6.
conditionalProb.hint: First form the posterior over the dice, then average the future 6-probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: Update the posterior over the candidate dice using prior × likelihood. a fair die: 1 × 0.027778 = 0.027778; a die with P(6) = 1/3: 1 × 0.111111 = 0.111111; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 After normalization, a fair die has posterior 0.0714; a die with P(6) = 1/3 has posterior 0.2857; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.6429 Averaging the probability of future sequence 6 and 6 under that posterior gives 0.1944.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen with prior weights 3:1 from a fair die and a die with P(6) = 1/3. You roll the chosen die and observe a 6. Estimate the probability the next roll is a 6.
conditionalProb.hint: First form the posterior over the dice, then average the future 6-probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: Update the posterior over the candidate dice using prior × likelihood. a fair die: 3 × 0.166667 = 0.5; a die with P(6) = 1/3: 1 × 0.333333 = 0.333333 After normalization, a fair die has posterior 0.6; a die with P(6) = 1/3 has posterior 0.4 Averaging the probability of future sequence 6 under that posterior gives 0.2333.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen with prior weights 1:3 from a fair die and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe not 6. Estimate the probability the next roll is a 6.
conditionalProb.hint: First form the posterior over the dice, then average the future 6-probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: Update the posterior over the candidate dice using prior × likelihood. a fair die: 1 × 0.833333 = 0.833333; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 3 × 0.5 = 1.5 After normalization, a fair die has posterior 0.3571; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.6429 Averaging the probability of future sequence 6 under that posterior gives 0.381.

conditionalProb.prompt: An urn is chosen uniformly from bag A (2 red, 1 blue) and bag B (1 red, 2 blue). You then draw without replacement and observe red and blue. Estimate the probability the next draw is red.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the urns, then average the next-draw color probability across the remaining urn compositions.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior using prior × exact without-replacement likelihood. bag A (2 red, 1 blue): 1 × 0.333333 = 0.333333; bag B (1 red, 2 blue): 1 × 0.333333 = 0.333333 After normalization, bag A (2 red, 1 blue) has posterior 0.5; bag B (1 red, 2 blue) has posterior 0.5 In each urn, update the remaining composition after the observed draws and compute the next-draw red probability. Averaging those probabilities under the posterior gives 0.5.

conditionalProb.prompt: An urn is chosen uniformly from bag A (3 red, 1 blue) and bag B (1 red, 3 blue). You then draw without replacement and observe red and red. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen urn was bag A (3 red, 1 blue).
conditionalProb.hint: Because draws are without replacement, the likelihood is a product of changing fractions.
conditionalProb.explanation: For each urn, compute the exact without-replacement likelihood of the observed color sequence, then multiply by the prior weight. bag A (3 red, 1 blue): 1 × 0.5 = 0.5; bag B (1 red, 3 blue): 1 × 0 = 0 Normalizing gives bag A (3 red, 1 blue) has posterior 1; bag B (1 red, 3 blue) has posterior 0 Therefore the posterior probability of bag A (3 red, 1 blue) is 1.

conditionalProb.prompt: An urn is chosen uniformly from bag A (3 red, 1 blue), bag B (2 red, 2 blue), and bag C (1 red, 3 blue). You then draw without replacement and observe red and blue. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen urn was bag B (2 red, 2 blue).
conditionalProb.hint: Because draws are without replacement, the likelihood is a product of changing fractions.
conditionalProb.explanation: For each urn, compute the exact without-replacement likelihood of the observed color sequence, then multiply by the prior weight. bag A (3 red, 1 blue): 1 × 0.25 = 0.25; bag B (2 red, 2 blue): 1 × 0.333333 = 0.333333; bag C (1 red, 3 blue): 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 Normalizing gives bag A (3 red, 1 blue) has posterior 0.3; bag B (2 red, 2 blue) has posterior 0.4; bag C (1 red, 3 blue) has posterior 0.3 Therefore the posterior probability of bag B (2 red, 2 blue) is 0.4.

conditionalProb.prompt: An urn is chosen with prior weights 2:1 from bag A (3 red, 1 blue) and bag B (2 red, 2 blue). You then draw without replacement and observe red. Estimate the probability the next draw is red.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the urns, then average the next-draw color probability across the remaining urn compositions.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior using prior × exact without-replacement likelihood. bag A (3 red, 1 blue): 2 × 0.75 = 1.5; bag B (2 red, 2 blue): 1 × 0.5 = 0.5 After normalization, bag A (3 red, 1 blue) has posterior 0.75; bag B (2 red, 2 blue) has posterior 0.25 In each urn, update the remaining composition after the observed draws and compute the next-draw red probability. Averaging those probabilities under the posterior gives 0.5833.

conditionalProb.prompt: An urn is chosen uniformly from bag A (4 red, 1 blue) and bag B (2 red, 3 blue). You then draw without replacement and observe blue and red. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen urn was bag B (2 red, 3 blue).
conditionalProb.hint: Because draws are without replacement, the likelihood is a product of changing fractions.
conditionalProb.explanation: For each urn, compute the exact without-replacement likelihood of the observed color sequence, then multiply by the prior weight. bag A (4 red, 1 blue): 1 × 0.2 = 0.2; bag B (2 red, 3 blue): 1 × 0.3 = 0.3 Normalizing gives bag A (4 red, 1 blue) has posterior 0.4; bag B (2 red, 3 blue) has posterior 0.6 Therefore the posterior probability of bag B (2 red, 3 blue) is 0.6.

conditionalProb.prompt: An urn is chosen with prior weights 1:2 from bag A (2 red, 2 blue) and bag B (1 red, 3 blue). You then draw without replacement and observe blue. Estimate the probability the next draw is blue.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the urns, then average the next-draw color probability across the remaining urn compositions.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior using prior × exact without-replacement likelihood. bag A (2 red, 2 blue): 1 × 0.5 = 0.5; bag B (1 red, 3 blue): 2 × 0.75 = 1.5 After normalization, bag A (2 red, 2 blue) has posterior 0.25; bag B (1 red, 3 blue) has posterior 0.75 In each urn, update the remaining composition after the observed draws and compute the next-draw blue probability. Averaging those probabilities under the posterior gives 0.5833.

conditionalProb.prompt: An urn is chosen uniformly from bag A (3 red, 2 blue) and bag B (2 red, 3 blue). You then draw without replacement and observe red, blue, and blue. Estimate the probability the next draw is red.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the urns, then average the next-draw color probability across the remaining urn compositions.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior using prior × exact without-replacement likelihood. bag A (3 red, 2 blue): 1 × 0.1 = 0.1; bag B (2 red, 3 blue): 1 × 0.2 = 0.2 After normalization, bag A (3 red, 2 blue) has posterior 0.3333; bag B (2 red, 3 blue) has posterior 0.6667 In each urn, update the remaining composition after the observed draws and compute the next-draw red probability. Averaging those probabilities under the posterior gives 0.6667.

conditionalProb.prompt: An urn is chosen uniformly from bag A (4 red, 1 blue), bag B (3 red, 2 blue), and bag C (2 red, 3 blue). You then draw without replacement and observe red and red. Estimate the probability the next draw is red.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the urns, then average the next-draw color probability across the remaining urn compositions.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior using prior × exact without-replacement likelihood. bag A (4 red, 1 blue): 1 × 0.6 = 0.6; bag B (3 red, 2 blue): 1 × 0.3 = 0.3; bag C (2 red, 3 blue): 1 × 0.1 = 0.1 After normalization, bag A (4 red, 1 blue) has posterior 0.6; bag B (3 red, 2 blue) has posterior 0.3; bag C (2 red, 3 blue) has posterior 0.1 In each urn, update the remaining composition after the observed draws and compute the next-draw red probability. Averaging those probabilities under the posterior gives 0.5.

conditionalProb.prompt: A deck is chosen uniformly from a standard 52-card deck and a short 32-card deck with 4 aces. You draw without replacement and observe an ace. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen deck was a short 32-card deck with 4 aces.
conditionalProb.hint: For each deck, compute prior × exact without-replacement likelihood of the observed card pattern, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: For each possible deck, compute the exact without-replacement likelihood of the observed pattern, then multiply by the prior weight. a standard 52-card deck: 1 × 0.076923 = 0.076923; a short 32-card deck with 4 aces: 1 × 0.125 = 0.125 Normalizing gives a standard 52-card deck has posterior 0.381; a short 32-card deck with 4 aces has posterior 0.619 So the posterior probability of a short 32-card deck with 4 aces is 0.619.

conditionalProb.prompt: A deck is chosen uniformly from a standard 52-card deck and a 20-card deck with 4 aces and 16 non-aces. You draw without replacement and observe an ace. Estimate the probability the next card is an ace.
conditionalProb.hint: First update the posterior over the possible decks, then average the next-card probability under the remaining composition of each deck.
conditionalProb.explanation: Update the posterior over the decks using prior × exact without-replacement likelihood. a standard 52-card deck: 1 × 0.076923 = 0.076923; a 20-card deck with 4 aces and 16 non-aces: 1 × 0.2 = 0.2 After normalization, a standard 52-card deck has posterior 0.2778; a 20-card deck with 4 aces and 16 non-aces has posterior 0.7222 Then compute the next-card probability of an ace in each remaining deck composition and average using the posterior weights. That gives 0.1304.

conditionalProb.prompt: A deck is chosen uniformly from a standard 52-card deck and a king-rich 52-card deck with 8 kings and 44 other cards. You draw without replacement and observe a king. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen deck was a king-rich 52-card deck with 8 kings and 44 other cards.
conditionalProb.hint: For each deck, compute prior × exact without-replacement likelihood of the observed card pattern, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: For each possible deck, compute the exact without-replacement likelihood of the observed pattern, then multiply by the prior weight. a standard 52-card deck: 1 × 0.076923 = 0.076923; a king-rich 52-card deck with 8 kings and 44 other cards: 1 × 0.153846 = 0.153846 Normalizing gives a standard 52-card deck has posterior 0.3333; a king-rich 52-card deck with 8 kings and 44 other cards has posterior 0.6667 So the posterior probability of a king-rich 52-card deck with 8 kings and 44 other cards is 0.6667.

conditionalProb.prompt: A deck is chosen uniformly from a standard 52-card deck and a red-heavy 52-card deck with 40 red cards and 12 black cards. You draw without replacement and observe a red card. Estimate the probability the next card is a red card.
conditionalProb.hint: First update the posterior over the possible decks, then average the next-card probability under the remaining composition of each deck.
conditionalProb.explanation: Update the posterior over the decks using prior × exact without-replacement likelihood. a standard 52-card deck: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5; a red-heavy 52-card deck with 40 red cards and 12 black cards: 1 × 0.769231 = 0.769231 After normalization, a standard 52-card deck has posterior 0.3939; a red-heavy 52-card deck with 40 red cards and 12 black cards has posterior 0.6061 Then compute the next-card probability of a red card in each remaining deck composition and average using the posterior weights. That gives 0.6566.

conditionalProb.prompt: A deck is chosen uniformly from a standard 52-card deck and a red-heavy 52-card deck with 40 red cards and 12 black cards. You draw without replacement and observe a red card and a red card. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen deck was a red-heavy 52-card deck with 40 red cards and 12 black cards.
conditionalProb.hint: For each deck, compute prior × exact without-replacement likelihood of the observed card pattern, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: For each possible deck, compute the exact without-replacement likelihood of the observed pattern, then multiply by the prior weight. a standard 52-card deck: 1 × 0.245098 = 0.245098; a red-heavy 52-card deck with 40 red cards and 12 black cards: 1 × 0.588235 = 0.588235 Normalizing gives a standard 52-card deck has posterior 0.2941; a red-heavy 52-card deck with 40 red cards and 12 black cards has posterior 0.7059 So the posterior probability of a red-heavy 52-card deck with 40 red cards and 12 black cards is 0.7059.

conditionalProb.prompt: A deck is chosen with prior weights 2:1 from a standard 52-card deck and a face-heavy 52-card deck with 20 face cards and 32 other cards. You draw without replacement and observe a face card. Estimate the probability the next card is a face card.
conditionalProb.hint: First update the posterior over the possible decks, then average the next-card probability under the remaining composition of each deck.
conditionalProb.explanation: Update the posterior over the decks using prior × exact without-replacement likelihood. a standard 52-card deck: 2 × 0.230769 = 0.461538; a face-heavy 52-card deck with 20 face cards and 32 other cards: 1 × 0.384615 = 0.384615 After normalization, a standard 52-card deck has posterior 0.5455; a face-heavy 52-card deck with 20 face cards and 32 other cards has posterior 0.4545 Then compute the next-card probability of a face card in each remaining deck composition and average using the posterior weights. That gives 0.287.

conditionalProb.prompt: A machine is chosen uniformly from a source with defect probability 1/4 and a source with defect probability 3/4. You inspect 4 independent items and see 3 defects. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen machine was a source with defect probability 3/4.
conditionalProb.hint: For each machine, use the binomial likelihood of the observed defect count, multiply by the prior weight, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are prior × binomial likelihood. a source with defect probability 1/4: 1 × 0.046875 = 0.046875; a source with defect probability 3/4: 1 × 0.421875 = 0.421875 Normalizing gives a source with defect probability 1/4 has posterior 0.1; a source with defect probability 3/4 has posterior 0.9 Therefore the posterior probability of a source with defect probability 3/4 is 0.9.

conditionalProb.prompt: A machine is chosen uniformly from a source with defect probability 1/4 and a source with defect probability 3/4. You inspect 4 independent items and see 3 defects. Estimate the probability the next inspected item is defective.
conditionalProb.hint: First update the posterior over the defect rates, then average the next-item defect probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the candidate defect rates. a source with defect probability 1/4: 1 × 0.046875 = 0.046875; a source with defect probability 3/4: 1 × 0.421875 = 0.421875 After normalization, a source with defect probability 1/4 has posterior 0.1; a source with defect probability 3/4 has posterior 0.9 Then average the defect probability of the next item under that posterior. That gives 0.7.

conditionalProb.prompt: A machine is chosen with prior weights 2:1 from a source with defect probability 1/4 and a source with defect probability 1/2. You inspect 2 independent items and see 2 defects. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen machine was a source with defect probability 1/2.
conditionalProb.hint: For each machine, use the binomial likelihood of the observed defect count, multiply by the prior weight, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are prior × binomial likelihood. a source with defect probability 1/4: 2 × 0.0625 = 0.125; a source with defect probability 1/2: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 Normalizing gives a source with defect probability 1/4 has posterior 0.3333; a source with defect probability 1/2 has posterior 0.6667 Therefore the posterior probability of a source with defect probability 1/2 is 0.6667.

conditionalProb.prompt: A machine is chosen uniformly from a source with defect probability 1/5, a source with defect probability 0.4, and a source with defect probability 0.6. You inspect 3 independent items and see 2 defects. Estimate the probability the next inspected item is defective.
conditionalProb.hint: First update the posterior over the defect rates, then average the next-item defect probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the candidate defect rates. a source with defect probability 1/5: 1 × 0.096 = 0.096; a source with defect probability 0.4: 1 × 0.288 = 0.288; a source with defect probability 0.6: 1 × 0.432 = 0.432 After normalization, a source with defect probability 1/5 has posterior 0.1176; a source with defect probability 0.4 has posterior 0.3529; a source with defect probability 0.6 has posterior 0.5294 Then average the defect probability of the next item under that posterior. That gives 0.4824.

conditionalProb.prompt: A model is chosen uniformly from a success probability 1/3 model and a success probability 2/3 model. In one experiment, the first success occurs on trial 3. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen model was a success probability 1/3 model.
conditionalProb.hint: For waiting time n, the geometric likelihood is p(1-p)^(n-1). Multiply by the prior and normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: For waiting time 3, the likelihood under success probability p is p(1-p)^2. a success probability 1/3 model: 1 × 0.148148 = 0.148148; a success probability 2/3 model: 1 × 0.074074 = 0.074074 Normalizing gives a success probability 1/3 model has posterior 0.6667; a success probability 2/3 model has posterior 0.3333 Therefore the posterior probability of a success probability 1/3 model is 0.6667.

conditionalProb.prompt: A model is chosen uniformly from a success probability 1/4 model, a success probability 1/2 model, and a success probability 3/4 model. In one experiment, the first success occurs on trial 2. Estimate the probability that a fresh new trial from the same chosen model succeeds immediately.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the success probabilities, then average p under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the candidate success probabilities. a success probability 1/4 model: 1 × 0.1875 = 0.1875; a success probability 1/2 model: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25; a success probability 3/4 model: 1 × 0.1875 = 0.1875 After normalization, a success probability 1/4 model has posterior 0.3; a success probability 1/2 model has posterior 0.4; a success probability 3/4 model has posterior 0.3 A fresh new trial succeeds immediately with probability p under each model, so the posterior predictive probability is the weighted average 0.5.

conditionalProb.prompt: A process is chosen uniformly from a Poisson process with rate 1 and a Poisson process with rate 2. Over a time window of length ln 2, you observe 0 arrivals. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen process was a Poisson process with rate 1.
conditionalProb.hint: Use the Poisson likelihood for the observed count in that window, multiply by the prior, and normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: For count 0 over horizon ln 2, the Poisson likelihood under rate λ is e^{-λt}(λt)^0 / 1. a Poisson process with rate 1: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5; a Poisson process with rate 2: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 Normalizing gives a Poisson process with rate 1 has posterior 0.6667; a Poisson process with rate 2 has posterior 0.3333 So the posterior probability of a Poisson process with rate 1 is 0.6667.

conditionalProb.prompt: A process is chosen uniformly from a Poisson process with rate 1 and a Poisson process with rate 2. Over a time window of length ln 2, you observe 0 arrivals. Estimate the probability that a fresh new time window of the same length contains zero arrivals.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the rates, then average e^{-λt} across the candidate rates.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the candidate rates. a Poisson process with rate 1: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5; a Poisson process with rate 2: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 After normalization, a Poisson process with rate 1 has posterior 0.6667; a Poisson process with rate 2 has posterior 0.3333 In a fresh window of length ln 2, a Poisson process with rate λ has probability e^{-λt} of zero arrivals. Averaging under the posterior gives 0.4167.

conditionalProb.prompt: A process is chosen uniformly from a Poisson process with rate 1 and a Poisson process with rate 3. Over a time window of length ln 2, you observe 0 arrivals. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen process was a Poisson process with rate 1.
conditionalProb.hint: Use the Poisson likelihood for the observed count in that window, multiply by the prior, and normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: For count 0 over horizon ln 2, the Poisson likelihood under rate λ is e^{-λt}(λt)^0 / 1. a Poisson process with rate 1: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5; a Poisson process with rate 3: 1 × 0.125 = 0.125 Normalizing gives a Poisson process with rate 1 has posterior 0.8; a Poisson process with rate 3 has posterior 0.2 So the posterior probability of a Poisson process with rate 1 is 0.8.

conditionalProb.prompt: A process is chosen uniformly from a Poisson process with rate 2 and a Poisson process with rate 4. Over a time window of length (ln 2) / 2, you observe 0 arrivals. Estimate the probability that a fresh new time window of the same length contains at least one arrival.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the rates, then average 1 - e^{-λt} across the candidate rates.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the candidate rates. a Poisson process with rate 2: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5; a Poisson process with rate 4: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 After normalization, a Poisson process with rate 2 has posterior 0.6667; a Poisson process with rate 4 has posterior 0.3333 In a fresh window of length (ln 2) / 2, a Poisson process with rate λ has probability 1 - e^{-λt} of at least one arrival. Averaging under the posterior gives 0.5833.

conditionalProb.prompt: A disease has prevalence 10%. A test has sensitivity 80% and false-positive rate 20%. A person gets test results positive. Estimate the posterior probability the person has the disease.
conditionalProb.hint: Posterior disease weight is prevalence × likelihood under disease. Posterior healthy weight is (1-prevalence) × likelihood under no disease.
conditionalProb.explanation: Under the disease state, the likelihood of results positive is 0.8^1 × 0.2^0, so the posterior disease weight is 0.08. Under the healthy state, the likelihood uses false positives for '+' and specificity for '-', giving posterior healthy weight 0.18. Normalizing yields posterior disease probability 0.3077.

conditionalProb.prompt: A disease has prevalence 5%. A test has sensitivity 90% and false-positive rate 10%. A person gets test results positive and positive. Estimate the posterior probability the person has the disease.
conditionalProb.hint: Posterior disease weight is prevalence × likelihood under disease. Posterior healthy weight is (1-prevalence) × likelihood under no disease.
conditionalProb.explanation: Under the disease state, the likelihood of results positive and positive is 0.9^2 × 0.1^0, so the posterior disease weight is 0.0405. Under the healthy state, the likelihood uses false positives for '+' and specificity for '-', giving posterior healthy weight 0.0095. Normalizing yields posterior disease probability 0.81.

conditionalProb.prompt: A disease has prevalence 2%. A test has sensitivity 95% and false-positive rate 5%. A person gets test results positive. Estimate the posterior probability the person has the disease.
conditionalProb.hint: Posterior disease weight is prevalence × likelihood under disease. Posterior healthy weight is (1-prevalence) × likelihood under no disease.
conditionalProb.explanation: Under the disease state, the likelihood of results positive is 0.95^1 × 0.05^0, so the posterior disease weight is 0.019. Under the healthy state, the likelihood uses false positives for '+' and specificity for '-', giving posterior healthy weight 0.049. Normalizing yields posterior disease probability 0.2794.

conditionalProb.prompt: A disease has prevalence 10%. A test has sensitivity 80% and false-positive rate 20%. A person gets test results positive and negative. Estimate the posterior probability the person has the disease.
conditionalProb.hint: Posterior disease weight is prevalence × likelihood under disease. Posterior healthy weight is (1-prevalence) × likelihood under no disease.
conditionalProb.explanation: Under the disease state, the likelihood of results positive and negative is 0.8^1 × 0.2^1, so the posterior disease weight is 0.016. Under the healthy state, the likelihood uses false positives for '+' and specificity for '-', giving posterior healthy weight 0.144. Normalizing yields posterior disease probability 0.1.

conditionalProb.prompt: A disease has prevalence 20%. A test has sensitivity 90% and false-positive rate 10%. A person gets test results positive. Estimate the probability a further independent test is positive.
conditionalProb.hint: First update the posterior disease probability from the observed tests, then average the positive-test probability under disease and no disease.
conditionalProb.explanation: The observed tests imply posterior disease weight 0.18 and posterior healthy weight 0.08, so the posterior disease probability is 0.6923. A further positive test then has probability posterior × sensitivity + (1 - posterior) × false-positive rate = 0.6538.

conditionalProb.prompt: A disease has prevalence 5%. A test has sensitivity 95% and false-positive rate 5%. A person gets test results positive and positive. Estimate the probability a further independent test is positive.
conditionalProb.hint: First update the posterior disease probability from the observed tests, then average the positive-test probability under disease and no disease.
conditionalProb.explanation: The observed tests imply posterior disease weight 0.045125 and posterior healthy weight 0.002375, so the posterior disease probability is 0.95. A further positive test then has probability posterior × sensitivity + (1 - posterior) × false-positive rate = 0.905.

conditionalProb.prompt: A disease has prevalence 1%. A test has sensitivity 99% and false-positive rate 1%. A person gets test results positive. Estimate the posterior probability the person has the disease.
conditionalProb.hint: Posterior disease weight is prevalence × likelihood under disease. Posterior healthy weight is (1-prevalence) × likelihood under no disease.
conditionalProb.explanation: Under the disease state, the likelihood of results positive is 0.99^1 × 0.01^0, so the posterior disease weight is 0.0099. Under the healthy state, the likelihood uses false positives for '+' and specificity for '-', giving posterior healthy weight 0.0099. Normalizing yields posterior disease probability 0.5.

conditionalProb.prompt: A disease has prevalence 15%. A test has sensitivity 85% and false-positive rate 15%. A person gets test results negative and negative. Estimate the posterior probability the person has the disease.
conditionalProb.hint: Posterior disease weight is prevalence × likelihood under disease. Posterior healthy weight is (1-prevalence) × likelihood under no disease.
conditionalProb.explanation: Under the disease state, the likelihood of results negative and negative is 0.85^0 × 0.15^2, so the posterior disease weight is 0.003375. Under the healthy state, the likelihood uses false positives for '+' and specificity for '-', giving posterior healthy weight 0.614125. Normalizing yields posterior disease probability 0.0055.

conditionalProb.prompt: A family has two children, each equally likely to be a boy or girl. Given that the older child is a boy, estimate the probability both children are boys.
conditionalProb.hint: Conditioning on the older child being a boy leaves only BB and BG.
conditionalProb.explanation: The four equally likely families are BB, BG, GB, and GG. Conditioning on the older child being a boy leaves only BB and BG. Exactly one of those two outcomes has both children boys, so the conditional probability is 1/2 = 0.5.

conditionalProb.prompt: A family has two children, each equally likely to be a boy or girl. Given that at least one child is a boy, estimate the probability both children are boys.
conditionalProb.hint: Conditioning on at least one boy removes only GG.
conditionalProb.explanation: The equally likely families are BB, BG, GB, and GG. Conditioning on at least one boy removes only GG, leaving BB, BG, and GB. Only one of those three surviving outcomes is BB, so the probability is 1/3 ≈ 0.3333.

conditionalProb.prompt: Three cards are equally likely: GG, GS, and SS, where G means a gold side and S means a silver side. You draw one card uniformly, place it on the table, and look at one random side. Given that the visible side is gold, estimate the probability the hidden side is also gold.
conditionalProb.hint: Count gold faces, not cards.
conditionalProb.explanation: The GG card contributes two gold faces, the GS card contributes one gold face, and the SS card contributes none. Conditional on seeing a gold face, the possible viewed faces are therefore GG-front, GG-back, and GS-gold. Two of those three cases come from GG, which means the hidden side is also gold with probability 2/3 ≈ 0.6667.

conditionalProb.prompt: In the standard 3-door Monty Hall problem, you choose one door uniformly, the host opens one different goat door, and you then switch to the other unopened door. Estimate the probability switching wins the car.
conditionalProb.hint: Your initial choice is right with probability 1/3 and wrong with probability 2/3.
conditionalProb.explanation: Your first pick is correct with probability 1/3 and wrong with probability 2/3. The host's action never transfers probability mass onto your first choice; it only concentrates the 2/3 wrong-initial-pick mass onto the one remaining unopened door. So switching wins with probability 2/3 ≈ 0.6667.

conditionalProb.prompt: There are 4 doors. One hides a prize and three hide goats. You choose one door uniformly, the host opens two different goat doors among the others, and you switch to the only other unopened door. Estimate the probability switching wins.
conditionalProb.hint: Your initial pick is right with probability 1/4 and wrong with probability 3/4.
conditionalProb.explanation: Your initial choice is correct with probability 1/4 and wrong with probability 3/4. If your initial choice is wrong, the only other unopened door after the host reveals two goats must be the prize door. So switching wins exactly when your first choice was wrong, which happens with probability 3/4 = 0.75.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen uniformly from a coin with P(H) = 1/3 and a coin with P(H) = 2/3. You then observe the toss sequence TT. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a coin with P(H) = 2/3.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/3: 1 × 0.444444 = 0.444444; a coin with P(H) = 2/3: 1 × 0.111111 = 0.111111 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/3 has posterior 0.8; a coin with P(H) = 2/3 has posterior 0.2 Therefore the posterior probability of a coin with P(H) = 2/3 is 0.2.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen uniformly from a coin with P(H) = 1/4, a fair coin, and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence TT. Estimate the probability the next 2 tosses are HH.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 1 × 0.5625 = 0.5625; a fair coin: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 1 × 0.0625 = 0.0625 After normalization, a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.6429; a fair coin has posterior 0.2857; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.0714 Then average the probability of future sequence HH under that posterior. That gives 0.1518.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 1:1:2 from an always-tail coin, a fair coin, and an always-heads coin. You then observe the toss sequence HHT. Estimate the probability the next 2 tosses are HH.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. an always-tail coin: 1 × 0 = 0; a fair coin: 1 × 0.125 = 0.125; an always-heads coin: 2 × 0 = 0 After normalization, an always-tail coin has posterior 0; a fair coin has posterior 1; an always-heads coin has posterior 0 Then average the probability of future sequence HH under that posterior. That gives 0.25.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen uniformly from a coin with P(H) = 1/4, a fair coin, and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence T. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a fair coin.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 1 × 0.75 = 0.75; a fair coin: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.5; a fair coin has posterior 0.3333; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.1667 Therefore the posterior probability of a fair coin is 0.3333.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen uniformly from a coin with P(H) = 1/4, a fair coin, and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence HT. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a coin with P(H) = 1/4.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 1 × 0.1875 = 0.1875; a fair coin: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 1 × 0.1875 = 0.1875 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.3; a fair coin has posterior 0.4; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.3 Therefore the posterior probability of a coin with P(H) = 1/4 is 0.3.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 1:1:2 from a coin with P(H) = 1/6, a fair coin, and a coin with P(H) = 5/6. You then observe the toss sequence H. Estimate the probability the next 2 tosses are HT.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. a coin with P(H) = 1/6: 1 × 0.166667 = 0.166667; a fair coin: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5; a coin with P(H) = 5/6: 2 × 0.833333 = 1.666667 After normalization, a coin with P(H) = 1/6 has posterior 0.0714; a fair coin has posterior 0.2143; a coin with P(H) = 5/6 has posterior 0.7143 Then average the probability of future sequence HT under that posterior. That gives 0.1627.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 1:2 from a coin with P(H) = 1/3 and a coin with P(H) = 2/3. You then observe the toss sequence TT. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a coin with P(H) = 1/3.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/3: 1 × 0.444444 = 0.444444; a coin with P(H) = 2/3: 2 × 0.111111 = 0.222222 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/3 has posterior 0.6667; a coin with P(H) = 2/3 has posterior 0.3333 Therefore the posterior probability of a coin with P(H) = 1/3 is 0.6667.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 1:2 from a coin with P(H) = 1/4 and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence T. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a coin with P(H) = 1/4.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 1 × 0.75 = 0.75; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 2 × 0.25 = 0.5 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.6; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.4 Therefore the posterior probability of a coin with P(H) = 1/4 is 0.6.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 2:1 from a coin with P(H) = 1/4 and a coin with P(H) = 3/4. You then observe the toss sequence HH. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a coin with P(H) = 3/4.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/4: 2 × 0.0625 = 0.125; a coin with P(H) = 3/4: 1 × 0.5625 = 0.5625 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/4 has posterior 0.1818; a coin with P(H) = 3/4 has posterior 0.8182 Therefore the posterior probability of a coin with P(H) = 3/4 is 0.8182.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 1:1:2 from a coin with P(H) = 1/6, a fair coin, and a coin with P(H) = 5/6. You then observe the toss sequence HT. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a fair coin.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/6: 1 × 0.138889 = 0.138889; a fair coin: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25; a coin with P(H) = 5/6: 2 × 0.138889 = 0.277778 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/6 has posterior 0.2083; a fair coin has posterior 0.375; a coin with P(H) = 5/6 has posterior 0.4167 Therefore the posterior probability of a fair coin is 0.375.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 1:1:2 from an always-tail coin, a fair coin, and an always-heads coin. You then observe the toss sequence HHT. Estimate the probability the next 2 tosses are HH.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. an always-tail coin: 1 × 0 = 0; a fair coin: 1 × 0.125 = 0.125; an always-heads coin: 2 × 0 = 0 After normalization, an always-tail coin has posterior 0; a fair coin has posterior 1; an always-heads coin has posterior 0 Then average the probability of future sequence HH under that posterior. That gives 0.25.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 2:1 from a coin with P(H) = 1/3 and a coin with P(H) = 2/3. You then observe the toss sequence H. Estimate the probability the next 2 tosses are HH.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. a coin with P(H) = 1/3: 2 × 0.333333 = 0.666667; a coin with P(H) = 2/3: 1 × 0.666667 = 0.666667 After normalization, a coin with P(H) = 1/3 has posterior 0.5; a coin with P(H) = 2/3 has posterior 0.5 Then average the probability of future sequence HH under that posterior. That gives 0.2778.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 1:2:1 from an always-tail coin, a fair coin, and an always-heads coin. You then observe the toss sequence H. Estimate the probability the next toss is T.
conditionalProb.hint: Update the posterior over the coins, then average the future-sequence probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: First update the posterior over the coins. an always-tail coin: 1 × 0 = 0; a fair coin: 2 × 0.5 = 1; an always-heads coin: 1 × 1 = 1 After normalization, an always-tail coin has posterior 0; a fair coin has posterior 0.5; an always-heads coin has posterior 0.5 Then average the probability of future sequence T under that posterior. That gives 0.25.

conditionalProb.prompt: A coin is chosen with prior weights 2:1 from a coin with P(H) = 1/3 and a coin with P(H) = 2/3. You then observe the toss sequence T. Estimate the posterior probability the chosen coin was a coin with P(H) = 2/3.
conditionalProb.hint: For each coin, compute prior × likelihood of the observed tosses, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The posterior weights are proportional to prior × likelihood. a coin with P(H) = 1/3: 2 × 0.666667 = 1.333333; a coin with P(H) = 2/3: 1 × 0.333333 = 0.333333 Normalizing gives a coin with P(H) = 1/3 has posterior 0.8; a coin with P(H) = 2/3 has posterior 0.2 Therefore the posterior probability of a coin with P(H) = 2/3 is 0.2.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen uniformly from a fair die, a die with P(6) = 1/3, and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe a 6 and a 6. Estimate the posterior probability the die was a fair die.
conditionalProb.hint: Treat “6” versus “not 6” exactly like heads versus tails: prior × likelihood, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The observation sequence only depends on each die through its probability of rolling a 6. So the posterior weights are prior × likelihood. a fair die: 1 × 0.027778 = 0.027778; a die with P(6) = 1/3: 1 × 0.111111 = 0.111111; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 Normalizing gives a fair die has posterior 0.0714; a die with P(6) = 1/3 has posterior 0.2857; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.6429 Hence the posterior probability of a fair die is 0.0714.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen with prior weights 1:2:1 from a fair die, a die with P(6) = 1/3, and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe a 6 and not 6. Estimate the probability the next 2 rolls are 6 and 6.
conditionalProb.hint: First form the posterior over the dice, then average the future 6-probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: Update the posterior over the candidate dice using prior × likelihood. a fair die: 1 × 0.138889 = 0.138889; a die with P(6) = 1/3: 2 × 0.222222 = 0.444444; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 1 × 0.25 = 0.25 After normalization, a fair die has posterior 0.1667; a die with P(6) = 1/3 has posterior 0.5333; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.3 Averaging the probability of future sequence 6 and 6 under that posterior gives 0.1389.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen with prior weights 1:2 from a fair die and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe not 6 and not 6. Estimate the probability the next 2 rolls are 6 and 6.
conditionalProb.hint: First form the posterior over the dice, then average the future 6-probability under that posterior.
conditionalProb.explanation: Update the posterior over the candidate dice using prior × likelihood. a fair die: 1 × 0.694444 = 0.694444; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 2 × 0.25 = 0.5 After normalization, a fair die has posterior 0.5814; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.4186 Averaging the probability of future sequence 6 and 6 under that posterior gives 0.1208.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen with prior weights 1:2 from a fair die and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe not 6 and not 6. Estimate the posterior probability the die was a fair die.
conditionalProb.hint: Treat “6” versus “not 6” exactly like heads versus tails: prior × likelihood, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The observation sequence only depends on each die through its probability of rolling a 6. So the posterior weights are prior × likelihood. a fair die: 1 × 0.694444 = 0.694444; a die with P(6) = 1/2: 2 × 0.25 = 0.5 Normalizing gives a fair die has posterior 0.5814; a die with P(6) = 1/2 has posterior 0.4186 Hence the posterior probability of a fair die is 0.5814.

conditionalProb.prompt: A die is chosen uniformly from a fair die, a die with P(6) = 1/3, and a die with P(6) = 1/2. You roll the chosen die and observe a 6 and a 6. Estimate the posterior probability the die was a die with P(6) = 1/2.
conditionalProb.hint: Treat “6” versus “not 6” exactly like heads versus tails: prior × likelihood, then normalize.
conditionalProb.explanation: The observation sequence only depends on each die through its probability of rolling a 6.
